introSection: Time Warp (formerly known as Tomb Raider: The Ride) was a steel flying roller coaster previously located at Canada's Wonderland in Vaughan, Ontario, Canada. It was Canada's first and only flying roller coaster. From its opening in 2004 to 2007, the coaster was known as Tomb Raider: The Ride.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride first opened on 2 May 2004 as Tomb Raider: The Ride, based on the 2001 Paramount film Lara Croft: Tomb Raider and the titular video game franchise.

After the sale of Paramount Parks to Cedar Fair, the company decided not to sign a new contract to use Paramount IP. As such, for the 2008 season, the ride was renamed to Time Warp. No significant changes were made to the coaster itself, only the sign at the entrance and the ride's queue line theming were modified.

The ride was removed from the park's app in February 2025. The park later confirmed its removal. The ride was demolished and scrapped in late March 2025.

As of May 2025, the entrance arch and station remain. The ride's sign and one of its cars are now part of the National Roller Coaster Museum in Plainview, Texas, USA.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
The ride was built by Zamperla and installed by Martin & Vleminckx.

It had a spiral lift hill and yellow track with turquoise supports. The trains in the station were always moving and did not stop. Time Warp's restraint system consisted of padding that rested right next to riders' heads. There was also a chin rest below riders' chins. Riders could hold on to handlebars. Riders stood in metal cages and rested their feet on one of five metal rungs. A ride operator would shut the metal guard on top of each rider. Riders then lifted into a flying position.

After ascending the spiral lift hill, riders navigated a course of tight twists, turns, dives, and inversions.

fullText:
Time Warp (formerly known as Tomb Raider: The Ride) was a steel flying roller coaster previously located at Canada's Wonderland in Vaughan, Ontario, Canada. It was Canada's first and only flying roller coaster. From its opening in 2004 to 2007, the coaster was known as Tomb Raider: The Ride.

The ride first opened on 2 May 2004 as Tomb Raider: The Ride, based on the 2001 Paramount film Lara Croft: Tomb Raider and the titular video game franchise.

After the sale of Paramount Parks to Cedar Fair, the company decided not to sign a new contract to use Paramount IP. As such, for the 2008 season, the ride was renamed to Time Warp. No significant changes were made to the coaster itself, only the sign at the entrance and the ride's queue line theming were modified.

The ride was removed from the park's app in February 2025. The park later confirmed its removal. The ride was demolished and scrapped in late March 2025.

As of May 2025, the entrance arch and station remain. The ride's sign and one of its cars are now part of the National Roller Coaster Museum in Plainview, Texas, USA.

The ride was built by Zamperla and installed by Martin & Vleminckx.

It had a spiral lift hill and yellow track with turquoise supports. The trains in the station were always moving and did not stop. Time Warp's restraint system consisted of padding that rested right next to riders' heads. There was also a chin rest below riders' chins. Riders could hold on to handlebars. Riders stood in metal cages and rested their feet on one of five metal rungs. A ride operator would shut the metal guard on top of each rider. Riders then lifted into a flying position.

After ascending the spiral lift hill, riders navigated a course of tight twists, turns, dives, and inversions.
